original_abstract: Abstract What are the unique features and characteristics of the scientists who have made the greatest discoveries in science? To address this question, we assess all major scientific discoverers, defined as all nobel-prize and major non-nobel-prize discoverers, and their demographic, institutional and economic traits. What emerges is a general profile of the scientists who have driven over 750 of science’s greatest advances. We find that interdisciplinary scientists who completed two or more degrees in different academic fields by the time of discovery made about half—54%—of all nobel-prize discoveries and 42% of major non-nobel-prize discoveries over the same period; this enables greater interdisciplinary methodological training for making new scientific achievements. Science is also becoming increasingly elitist, with scientists at the top 25 ranked universities accounting for 30% of both all nobel-prize and non-nobel-prize discoveries. Scientists over the age of 50 made only 7% of all nobel-prize discoveries and 15% of non-nobel-prize discoveries and those over the age of 60 made only 1% and 3%, respectively. The gap in years between making nobel-prize discoveries and receiving the award is also increasing over time across scientific fields—illustrating that it is taking longer to recognise and select major breakthroughs. Overall, we find that those who make major discoveries are increasingly interdisciplinary, older and at top universities. We also assess here the role and distribution of factors like geographic location, gender, religious affiliation and country conditions of these leading scientists, and how these factors vary across time and scientific fields. The findings suggest that more discoveries could be made if science agencies and research institutions provide greater incentives for researchers to work against the common trend of narrow specialisation and instead foster interdisciplinary research that combines novel methods across fields.
humanized_abstract: Can we describe the characteristics of the successful scientist? We examined the demographic, institutional and economic characteristics of all the major scientific discoverers, defined as all the nobel-prize and major non-nobel-prize discoverers. We then took a snapshot of these scientists and the scientists behind the 750 plus science’s greatest advances. We found that: - The large number of interdisciplinary scientists who completed two or more degrees in different academic fields by the time of the discovery made half - 54% of all the nobel-prize discoveries and 42% of the major non-nobel-prize discoveries. The greater methodological training across fields allows for more possibility of new discoveries; - Science is becoming elitist, with scientists at the top 25 ranked universities making 30% of all the nobel-prize and non-nobel-prize discoveries; - Older scientists are very rare among the makers of science’s greatest advances, with those over 50 accounting for only 7% of all the nobel-prize discoveries and 15% of the non-nobel-prize discoveries, and those over 60 only 1% and 3% respectively; - There has been a growing gap in years between making a nobel-prize discovery and winning the prize across nearly all fields of science. Hence our results indicate that the leading makers of science’s greatest advances tend to be interdisciplinary, older and in top universities. We also examined here the role of the following covariates in describing leading makers of science’s greatest advances: location, gender, religion and country characteristics over time and across scientific fields, and describe the trends in these characteristics. Our study provides insights to suggest that more discoveries are likely to be made if science agencies and research institutions provide stronger incentives for successful and talented scientists to counteract the forces that are leading to increased specialization and instead emphasize the importance of successful interdisciplinary research using novel methods and approaches across fields.
